coredata.dc:title: Degradation of ibuprofen in the carbon dots/Fe3O4@carbon sphere pomegranate-like composites activated persulfate system
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Bo-Tao
Wang, Qian
Zhang, Yang
Teng, Yanguo
Fan, Maohong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots and nanomagnetites encapsulated in amorphous carbon spheres (CDs/Fe3O4@CS) with a pomegranate-like structure were synthesized via a solvothermal method and their peroxide activation performances for ibuprofen degradation were investigated in this work. The pomegranate-like nanocomposites, with an average diameter of 451.9 nm, possessed multicores and a 20–50 nm thick amorphous carbon shell. The multicores were composed of multiple 3.9–9.8 nm carbon dots and 5.6–18.6 nm nanomagnetites, as confirmed by X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron, Raman and photoluminescence spectra. The Fe3O4 loading was calculated to be 50.9 wt% according to thermo-gravimetric analysis. CDs/Fe3O4@CS had a specific surface area of 101.5 m2/g and the saturation magnetization of 26.8 emu/g. The CDs/Fe3O4@CS formation mechanism was proposed based on the characterization results. CDs/Fe3O4@CS exhibited high catalytic activities for peroxymonosulfate, persulfate and H2O2 with and without visible light illumination, and persulfate showed higher ibuprofen degradation efficiencies. The amorphous carbon shell might benefit ibuprofen enrichment, the nano Fe3O4 provided sufficient reactive sites to activate persulfate, and the CDs could accelerate the degradation due to their excellent electron donating and accepting abilities. The ibuprofen degradation in the CDs/Fe3O4@CS activated persulfate system followed a pseudo-first-order kinetic model and the activation energy was derived as 27.2 kJ/mol. The CDs/Fe3O4@CS also presented good reusability performances in the repeated experiments with visible light illumination. Singlet oxygen, hydroxyl and sulfate radicals were verified and the roles of these reactive species in ibuprofen degradation pathways were discussed based on intermediate analysis. The pomegranate-like nanocomposites might provide a potential promising catalyst for pollution control technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanomagnetite
Pomegranate-like composites
Persulfate
Radicals
Ibuprofen

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical methods for ibuprofen, degradation intermediates and reactive species
Catalytic activities of CDs/Fe3O4@CS for ibuprofen degradation
Identification of reactive species and possible ibuprofen degradation pathways

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM image (a), particle size distribution (b), HRTEM image (c), and EDS mapping (d) of the CDs/Fe3O4@CS catalyst.
XRD (a), C 1s XPS (b), O 1s XPS (c) and Fe 3p XPS (d) spectra of CDs/Fe3O4@CS.
Raman spectrum (a), photoluminescence spectra (b), magnetic hysteresis loop (c) and TGA-DSC profiles (d) of CDs/Fe3O4@CS.
Schematic diagram of the CDs/Fe3O4@CS formation processes.

The ibuprofen degradation profiles by CDs/Fe3O4@CS activated peroxides with visible light (a) or without light (b) and removal curves by persulfate with visible light (c) or without light (d) under different conditions. Peroxides, 1 mmol/L; Ibuprofen, 50 μmol/L; Solids, 0.3 g/L.

Diagram of ln(C/C0) versus time for ibuprofen degradation rate calculated by the pseudo-first-order kinetic model and Arrhenius plot of ibuprofen degradation (insert figure). Persulfate, 1 mmol/L; Ibuprofen, 50 μmol/L; CDs/Fe3O4@CS, 0.3 g/L.
Reusability of CDs/Fe3O4@CS for ibuprofen degradation with visible light. Persulfate, 1 mmol/L; Ibuprofen, 50 μmol/L; CDs/Fe3O4@CS, 0.3 g/L.

ESR spectra of CDs/Fe3O4@CS activated persulfate with visible light (a) or without light illumination (b). (1) DMPO·OH; (2) DMPO·-SO4 - with excess TBA; (3) TEMPO. DMPO, 0.1 mol/L; TEMP, 0.05 mol/L; TBA, 0.5 mol/L; Persulfate, 1 mmol/L; CDs/Fe3O4@CS, 0.3 g/L.
LC-MS total ion chromatogram in ESI positive (a) and negative (b) ion model of ibuprofen degradation intermediates.
Possible ibuprofen degradation pathways by CDs/Fe3O4@CS activated persulfate.

head.ce:title: Degradation of ibuprofen in the carbon dots/Fe3O4@carbon sphere pomegranate-like composites activated persulfate system

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Water Sciences, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Petroleum Engineering, University of Wyoming, Laramie, WY 82071, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Petroleum Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Petroleum Engineering, University of Wyoming, Laramie, WY 82071, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots and nanomagnetites encapsulated in amorphous carbon spheres (CDs/Fe3O4@CS) with a pomegranate-like structure were synthesized via a solvothermal method and their peroxide activation performances for ibuprofen degradation were investigated in this work. The pomegranate-like nanocomposites, with an average diameter of 451.9 nm, possessed multicores and a 20–50 nm thick amorphous carbon shell. The multicores were composed of multiple 3.9–9.8 nm carbon dots and 5.6–18.6 nm nanomagnetites, as confirmed by X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron, Raman and photoluminescence spectra. The Fe3O4 loading was calculated to be 50.9 wt% according to thermo-gravimetric analysis. CDs/Fe3O4@CS had a specific surface area of 101.5 m2/g and the saturation magnetization of 26.8 emu/g. The CDs/Fe3O4@CS formation mechanism was proposed based on the characterization results. CDs/Fe3O4@CS exhibited high catalytic activities for peroxymonosulfate, persulfate and H2O2 with and without visible light illumination, and persulfate showed higher ibuprofen degradation efficiencies. The amorphous carbon shell might benefit ibuprofen enrichment, the nano Fe3O4 provided sufficient reactive sites to activate persulfate, and the CDs could accelerate the degradation due to their excellent electron donating and accepting abilities. The ibuprofen degradation in the CDs/Fe3O4@CS activated persulfate system followed a pseudo-first-order kinetic model and the activation energy was derived as 27.2 kJ/mol. The CDs/Fe3O4@CS also presented good reusability performances in the repeated experiments with visible light illumination. Singlet oxygen, hydroxyl and sulfate radicals were verified and the roles of these reactive species in ibuprofen degradation pathways were discussed based on intermediate analysis. The pomegranate-like nanocomposites might provide a potential promising catalyst for pollution control technologies.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfate radical based advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have been extensively investigated in the last two decades since sulfate radical has several advantages over hydroxyl radical in the degradation or mineralization of traditional and emerging contaminants [1,2]. Sulfate radical and other reactive species can be generated in activated peroxymonosulfate or persulfate systems through peroxide bond scission by energy or transition metal based catalysts [3]. Heterogeneous catalysts for peroxymonosulfate or persulfate activation have become a popular research area to reduce costs and avoid the adverse influences of heavy metals [4,5]. However, several challenges still exist for the practical application of the heterogeneous catalysts, such as heavy metal leakage and difficult separation [6].

Carbon nanoallotropes are a broad family of materials, and most of them have demonstrated promises as catalysts in the cleaner remediation technologies to overcome metal leaching problem of metal-based AOPs [6,7]. Nanocarbons with different dimensions, such as 0D fullerene, 1D carbon nanotubes or carbon nanofibers, 2D graphene or g-C3N4 and 3D mesoporous carbon, have been used for the metal-free activation of peroxymonosulfate and persulfate because of their large surface area, unique electronic properties, and sp2-hybridized carbon configuration [8,9].

Zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs), a new type of carbon nanomaterial with sizes below 10 nm, which were firstly obtained during the purification of carbon nanotubes in 2004 [10], have gradually become a rising star due to their robust chemical inertness, good biocompatibility, easy functionalization and inexpensive nature. CDs have been used in a series of exciting application areas, such as bioimaging, drug delivery, sensors, catalysis, and photovoltaic devices [7,11]. CDs can exhibit the excellent tunable photoluminescence and electron transfer/reservoir properties, so they have been successfully introduced into semiconductors to improve the photocatalytic performance in pollutant degradation (almost exclusive organic dyes), CO2 reduction, hydrogen production, and organic synthesis [12,13]. Nevertheless, few papers have been dedicated to the application of CDs in sulfate radical based AOPs according to the literature survey. Reusability might be a significant issue for the practical application of CDs since they cannot be easily separated from the reaction solution due to their nanosize and low solubility in water.

Core-shell and yolk-shell nanoparticles with the magnetic response have attracted intensive interest because the permeable shell can protect the core from aggregation or erosion and allow the diffusion of small active molecules crossing the nano structures. The magnetic core-shell or yolk-shell nanoparticles have been applied in AOPs, such as Fe3O4@Fe3O4/C activated H2O2 [14], nickel ferrite@CS activated persulfate [15] and nano-Fe0@CS activated peroxymonosulfate [16]. Compared with simple core-shell nanoparticles, pomegranate-like nanocomposites can afford more active sites and show better catalytic activities because of their unique structure with multiple cores. Multifunctional pomegranate-like nanocomposites can also combine the virtues of their different components to be nano-reactors [17]. These promising nanocomposites have been used in various application, such as highly active electrocatalysts [18] and photocatalysts [19].

Carbon dots and nanomagnetite encapsulated in amorphous carbon spheres (CDs/Fe3O4@CS) with a pomegranate-like structure were prepared by a solvothermal method in this work. The formation mechanism of the nanocomposites was proposed based on the characterization results. The contributions of the carbon shell, CDs and nano Fe3O4 multicores to ibuprofen degradation by activated peroxides were explored, and the kinetic and thermodynamic parameters were investigated. The reactive species in the CDs/Fe3O4@CS activated persulfate system were detected and the ibuprofen degradation pathways were proposed according to intermediate analysis. The pomegranate-like nanocomposites, such as CDs/Fe3O4@CS, have the potential in the pollution control applications.

Ferrocene, ibuprofen sodium and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Louis, MO, USA). Sodium persulfate was obtained from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, USA). Acetone and hydrogen peroxide (30%) were from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). HPLC-grade acetonitrile and methanol were acquired from J. T. Baker (Phillipsburg, NJ, USA), and HPLC-grade formic acid was bought from Dikma Technologies Inc. (Richmond Hill, ON, USA). 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine-N-oxide (DMPO) was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The quenching reagent tert-butyl alcohol (TBA) was from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China).

CDs/Fe3O4@CS catalysts were synthesized via a solvothermal method using ferrocene and hydrogen peroxide. Typically, 1.00 g ferrocene was dissolved in 30 mL acetone to form a clean solution through intense sonication. Then, 6.0 mL 30% H2O2 was added drop-wise with vigorous stirring and the mixture solution was stirred continuously for another 20 min. The obtained solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, sealed and heated at 200 °C for 48 h. After cooling to room temperature, the products were collected using an external magnet and washed with acetone and purified water to remove impurities. Finally, the brown-black solid products were dried for subsequent use. CDs and Fe3O4 nanoparticles (nFe3O4) were also synthesized for comparison with the CDs/Fe3O4@CS catalysts. The detailed synthesis methods are available in the Supplementary data.

The morphology and elemental mapping were investigated by a JEM 2100F high resolution transmission electron microscopy coupled with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (HRTEM-EDS, Jeol, Tokyo, Japan) at a 200 kV acceleration voltage. The size distribution was determined by a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 particle size analyzer (Malvern, UK). The crystallinity was investigated by an X'Pert PRO MPD X-ray diffraction (XRD) instrument (PANalytical BV, Almelo, Netherlands). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded to analyze the elemental compositions and surface chemical states using an ESCALAB 250XI X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., MA, USA) with Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV) under 5 × 10−9 mbar. Raman spectra were obtained by a LabRAM Aramis confocal laser micro-Raman spectrometer (HJY, Paris, France). The thermal behaviors were characterized by an STA 449 F3 Jupiter thermo-gravimetric analysis-differential scanning calorimetry (TGA-DSC, Netzsch Company, Selb, Germany). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured on a steady-state fluorescence spectrofluorometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were conducted using a Quadrasorb SI analyzer (Quantachrome Instruments, Boynton Beach, FL, USA). A vibration sample magnetometer (7410-VSM, LakeShore, Westerville, OH, USA) was used to study the magnetic responses of the obtained materials.

Batch ibuprofen degradation experiments were conducted to investigate the catalytic performances of the catalysts. A certain concentration of ibuprofen stock solution and deionized water were added into reactors with stirring. The catalysts and peroxide (peroxymonosulfate, persulfate or H2O2) were introduced to the reaction solution, successively. The samples were taken out from at a certain interval and treated with disposable syringe filters for following analysis. The photocatalytic experiments were conducted in a multi-tube stirred photoreactor (XPA-System, Xujiang Electromechanical Plant, Nanjing, China), which composed of a 350 W xenon lamp as a light source with glass filters (λ > 420 nm). Each experiment was conducted for three times and the mean values were illustrated in figures with error bars (relative standard deviations).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical methods for ibuprofen, degradation intermediates and reactive species
ce:section.ce:para: The ibuprofen concentration was analyzed using a high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC-20A, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan), equipped with a diode array detector. A C18 column (5 μm, 4.6 mm × 250 mm, WondaSil, GL Sciences Inc., Kyoto, Japan) was used for separation. The mobile phase was a mixture of 63% acetonitrile and 37% water containing 0.1% formic acid with the flow rate of 1.0 mL/min.

The degradation intermediates were identified using a SCIEX ExionLC AD-X500R QTOF-MS/MS system (QTOF-X500R, SCIEX Co., Singapore). A Kinetex C18 column (2.6 µm, 2.1 mm × 100 mm, Phenomenex, Torrance, CA, USA) was used for chromatographic separation. The spray voltage of the electrospray ionization (ESI) was 5500 V (ESI+) and −4500 V (ESI-). The curtain gas was 30 psi, and the accumulation time was 0.2 s.

A Freiberg MS-5000 electron spin resonance instrument (ESR, Freiberg, Germany) was applied to investigate the reactive species in different degradation systems. The ESR instrument was operated under the chosen conditions (modulation amplitude, 0.20 mT; modulation frequency, 100 kHz; sweep width, 25 mT; sweep time, 60 s; microwave power, 10.00 mW; field modulation, 0.2 mT).

Ferrocene has been used as a versatile precursor to synthesize nanotubes and carbon-encapsulated nanoparticles since it can provide both iron and carbon sources [20,21]. Ferrocene was also selected as the precursor in this work to obtain highly efficient catalysts for peroxide activation. The morphology and microstructure of the obtained materials were firstly investigated. The synthesized nanocomposites show a typical pomegranate-like structure according to the TEM image (Fig. 1 a), and possess multicores with a single 20–50 nm thick shell. The average diameter of the nanocomposites is 451.9 nm (Fig. 1b). The crystalline composition of the different sections was further elucidated by HRTEM. The multicores have evident lattice fringes according to Fig. 1c. The interplanar distance of 0.253 nm corresponds to the (3 1 1) lattice planes of Fe3O4, while the interplanar distance of 0.335 nm results from the (0 0 6) lattice planes of sp2 carbon [22]. Therefore, the multicores were inferred to be composed of individual nanomagnetite crystalline particles with diameters of 5.6–18.6 nm and crystallographic carbon dots with diameters of 3.9–9.8 nm. More than 88% carbon accounts for the element composition of the external layer of the nano-spheres (Fig. S1) and there is no clear lattice fringe, so the single shell is deduced to be amorphous carbon. The element distribution and composition of the synthesized materials were given in EDS mapping (Fig. 1d). It is clear from Fig. 1d that carbon (C) is distributed in both shell and core areas, and the strong Fe and O signals appear at the core of the nanoparticle. Therefore, the pomegranate-like nanocomposites were preliminarily inferred to be carbon dots and nanomagnetites encapsulated in amorphous carbon spheres, which were denoted as CDs/Fe3O4@CS.

The XRD pattern of CDs/Fe3O4@CS is shown in Fig. 2 a. CDs/Fe3O4@CS exhibit six characteristic reflection peaks at 30.1°, 35.4°, 43.1°, 53.4°, 57.0°, and 62.5°, which correspond to the (2 2 0), (3 1 1), (4 0 0), (4 2 2), (5 1 1), and (4 4 0) planes, respectively, of the magnetite (Fe3O4) phase (JCPDS file No. 19-0629) [23]. The elemental composition and surface chemical states of the CDs/Fe3O4@CS were also investigated by XPS analysis. The full-scan XPS spectrum of the CDs/Fe3O4@CS is composed of the elements of C, O and Fe (Fig. S2a). The C 1s, O 1s, Fe 2p and Fe 3p XPS spectra were further deconvoluted and are shown in Fig. 2b, Fig. 2c, Fig. S2b and Fig. 2d, respectively. The C 1s XPS spectrum can be divided into a prominent peak at 284.9 eV and a well-defined shoulder at 288.5 eV, which could be attributed to the CC bonds and carbonyl groups (>CO), respectively. The O 1s spectrum consists of three peaks at 530.3, 531.8 and 533.7 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen (MO) in iron oxides, hydroxyl groups (OH) and the oxygen in adsorbed water (H2O), respectively [24–26]. The Fe 2p spectrum consists of two peaks at 711.3 and 724.8 eV, due to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 spin-orbital components, respectively (Fig. S2b). The Fe 3p peak is a single peak without any interfering satellite peaks, so it is always deconvoluted for the quantitative analysis of Fe(III) and Fe(II) [27]. The Fe 3p XPS spectra were deconvoluted to Fe(III) and Fe(II) peaks and the area ratio of Fe(III):Fe(II) peaks was calculated as 2.02, which is consistent with the stoichiometric ratio of Fe(III) and Fe(II) for Fe3O4 (Fig. 2d). The characteristic peaks of the O 1 s and Fe 3p XPS spectra indicate the existence of magnetite.

The typical Raman spectra of most carbon nanoallotropes exhibit a broad graphitic band (G band) at approximately 1550–1600 cm−1 and a broad peak (D band) at about 1320–1345 cm−1. The G band is associated with the vibration of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice. The D band is related to the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds [7]. There also exist a G band (1590 cm−1) and a D band (1331 cm−1) in the Raman spectrum of CDs/Fe3O4@CS according to Fig. 3 a. The intensity ratio of IG/ID is 1.227, which means that CDs/Fe3O4@CS contains more crystalline CDs than amorphous carbon.

The most characteristic and significant property of CDs is their photoluminescence (PL), which depends on their size, the surface functionalization and the excitation wavelength [28]. The relationship between the excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs/Fe3O4@CS is demonstrated in Fig. 3b. The maximum PL wavelength was progressively red-shifted from 587 nm to 876 nm with increasing excitation wavelength from 390 nm to 580 nm. The PL intensities have two peaks at 618 nm and 800 nm, which were obtained at the excitation wavelengths of 410 nm and 530 nm, respectively. These excitation wavelength-dependent behaviors, which were unique and quite different from those of other PL nanomaterials such as semiconductor quantum dots, further proved the existence of carbon dots in the nanocomposites. The multiemission feature of CDs is related to their surface energy traps/states, poly-aromatic structures, quantum confinement effect, electron-hole recombination, edge effect and oxygen-containing groups [29].

The magnetic properties of the as-prepared CDs/Fe3O4@CS were investigated by a magnetometer. The magnetic hysteresis loop of the nanocomposites at the maximum field strength of 18 kOe is shown in Fig. 3c. The saturation magnetization of CDs/Fe3O4@CS was determined to be 26.8 emu/g and the hysteresis loop also indicates that CDs/Fe3O4@CS possess superparamagnetic behavior with no remanence or coercivity at room temperature. This magnetic property is desirable for easy recycling by an external magnetic field.

The TGA-DSC profiles of CDs/Fe3O4@CS are displayed in Fig. 3d, which were conducted in an air atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C/min from room temperature. The mild weight loss stage below 220 °C might be attributed to the evaporation of water molecules and the removal of the labile oxygen-containing functional groups on the carbon surface [30]. A dramatic weight loss stage occurred from 220 °C to 320 °C and a distinct exothermic peak can be seen at 306 °C with a small shoulder at 263 °C in the DSC curve, which could be assigned to the decomposition and combustion of amorphous carbon and CDs. After the temperature was elevated above 400 °C, the weight loss was stabilized and finally determined to be 47.3%, indicating the complete combustion of carbon compounds and the oxidation of iron with the ultimate product Fe2O3 [16]. The Fe3O4 loading in CDs/Fe3O4@CS was calculated to be 50.9% by weight.

The nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm and pore size distribution of CDs/Fe3O4@CS are illustrated in Fig. S3. It was calculated that CDs/Fe3O4@CS had a surface area of 101.5 m2/g and a pore volume of 0.293 cm3/g. There was one peak centered at 1.9 nm in the pore size distribution figure (Fig. S3b). The porous structure and high surface area might endow CDs/Fe3O4@CS molecular diffusion channels, more reaction and adsorption sites.

A schematic diagram of the CDs/Fe3O4@CS formation processes was proposed based on the characterization results above and related literatures as shown in Fig. 4 . Ferrocene is decomposed by H2O2 to form iron atoms and cyclopentadiene or cyclopentadienyl radical at high temperature [31,32]. Fe2+ could be generated via two-electron transfer from zero-valent ion to H2O2. Fe3+, oxygen gas and other reactive species, such as hydroxyl radical, hydroperoxyl radical and superoxide radical, are generated through Fenton reactions. Magnetite is subsequently produced, then grows and agglomerates into larger nano clusters because of the supersaturated solution, high temperature and magnetism. Cyclopentadiene or cyclopentadienyl radical can be further decomposed or reorganized into different carbon-based radicals and fragments by H2O2 and the secondary reactive species. Iron oxides may promote the graphitization degree of carbon materials, so crystalline carbon dots are produced and grown among the nanomagnetite clusters in these processes. Finally, amorphous carbon grows as an external shell around the Fe3O4/CDs multicores from carbon intermediates (carbon radicals and/or fragments) to form the pomegranate-like nanocomposites at a high reaction temperature and high pressure from the generating oxygen and gasification of solvents in the reactor [33,34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activities of CDs/Fe3O4@CS for ibuprofen degradation
ce:section.ce:para: Preliminary experiments were conducted to compare the catalytic activities of CDs/Fe3O4@CS for three peroxides (peroxymonosulfate, persulfate or hydrogen peroxide) to degrade ibuprofen with visible light (a xenon lamp as the light source with glass filters, λ > 420 nm) and without light illumination, as shown in Fig. 5 a and 5b, respectively. It is interesting that pretty similar ibuprofen degradation efficiencies (from 96.0% for persulfate to 97.6% for peroxymonosulfate in 2 h) and oxidation reaction rates were obtained by the three peroxides activated by CDs/Fe3O4@CS with visible light illumination (Fig. 5a). Without visible light irradiation, the ibuprofen degradation efficiency of persulfate activated by CDs/Fe3O4@CS did not change greatly, decreasing from 96.0% to 94.5% in 2 h (Fig. 5b). However, the ibuprofen degradation efficiencies reduced to 88.4% and 87.1% by hydrogen peroxide and peroxymonosulfate, respectively. The ultraviolet (UV) instead of visible light was used to activate these peroxides because UV could provide sufficient energy to crack the peroxo bond and the absorbance of peroxides is in the UV region [3]. One of attractive properties of CDs is their upconversion characteristics, which emits low-wavelength light under the excitation of long wavelength light. The upconversion property enables the CD-based photo-catalysts to be workable under visible light induction [22]. CDs have been synthesized with other semiconductors photo-catalysts, such as TiO2 [35] and BiOBr [36], for degradations of organic contaminants under visible light. The performances of peroxide activation with visible light illumination were improved compared with that without light illumination, especially for H2O2 and peroxymonosulfate, which might be benefited by the upconversion characteristics of CDs to scissor the peroxo bonds. The improvement was not good as other systems [35,36] might be because the Fe3O4 is not a good catalyst and CDs in CDs/Fe3O4@CS have low concentration and upconversion efficiency. Persulfate was selected for subsequent investigation due to its higher degradation efficiencies under both conditions.

Ibuprofen removal by persulfate under different conditions was also conducted to compare the catalytic activities of CDs/Fe3O4@CS with that of other catalysts, such as nFe3O4 and CDs (Fig. S4). In the presence of persulfate only, 10.7% and 4.5% of ibuprofen were removed at 2 h with and without visible light as shown in Fig. 5c and 5d, respectively. It demonstrated that persulfate, with a redox potential of 2.01 V, could not efficiently oxidize ibuprofen and the scission of the peroxide bond could not be efficiently achieved to generate radicals under visible light [3]. Less than 7.0% ibuprofen was removed in the CDs/persulfate system, so the obtained CDs alone could not activate persulfate effectively. In the nFe3O4/persulfate system, 27.7% and 11.5% of ibuprofen were degraded at 2 h, with and without visible light illumination, respectively, and it similar to previous reports that nFe3O4 can be used as a persulfate activator [37,38]. In the absence of persulfate, 50.6% and 48.5% of ibuprofen was removed by CDs/Fe3O4@CS with and without visible light irradiation, respectively. It indicated that CDs/Fe3O4@CS can adsorb ibuprofen due to its high specific surface area and pore volume, and its photocatalytic activities under visible light for ibuprofen degradation can be neglected. Almost complete ibuprofen removal could be achieved at 2 h in the CDs/Fe3O4@CS activated persulfate system.

The mechanism of the high catalytic activity of CDs/Fe3O4@CS might be ascribed to the carbon spheres with high specific surface area and pore volume, and the multicores composed of nano magnetite and CDs crystalline particles. First, the porous structure of the carbon shell benefits the enrichment of ibuprofen from bulk solution and the fast diffusion toward the Fe3O4/CDs multicores, which is favorable for the heterogeneous catalytic reaction. Second, nFe3O4 particles bestrewn within the core provide sufficient reactive sites to activate persulfate to generate free radicals. Third, CDs particles grown among the nanomagnetite clusters could accelerate the degradation due to their excellent electron donating and accepting ability [14,16,39].

Ibuprofen degradation performances by persulfate at different CDs/Fe3O4@CS catalyst dosages and persulfate concentrations were investigated with visible light and without light illumination (Figs. S5 and S6). Higher CDs/Fe3O4@CS dosages resulted in higher ibuprofen degradation rates and efficiencies. Furthermore, higher ibuprofen removal efficiencies with visible light (83.9%) can also be achieved with 0.1 g/L catalyst, comparing with that (57.3%) without light. Ibuprofen degradation efficiencies and rates increased with persulfate concentration from 0.05 mmol/L to 5 mmol/L. More than 90% ibuprofen degradation was achieved at 2 h when the persulfate/ibuprofen molar ratio was above 10:1. When the persulfate concentration was lower than 0.2 mmol/L, ibuprofen degradation efficiencies without light radiation were higher than that with visible light. The reason might be that side reactions could occur to consume persulfate during photoreaction and become remarkable when the persulfate concentration was low.

The ibuprofen degradation was also investigated at different reaction temperature in range of 10–50 °C without light illumination. A higher temperature resulted in a higher oxidation efficiencies and degradation rates (Fig. S7). At 10 °C, only 44.9% of the ibuprofen was decomposed at 20 min, while 90.0% ibuprofen was removed in the same time when the reaction temperature was 50 °C. Pseudo-first-order kinetics was used to calculate the degradation rates:
In (
C 0
/
C t
) = k t
where Ct and C0 are the ibuprofen concentrations at time t and initial time, respectively, and k is the degradation rate constant. The ibuprofen degradation efficiencies and rates increased with reaction temperature, which followed a pseudo-first-order kinetic model with high correlation coefficients (r > 0.9088, Fig. 6 and Table S1). The activation energy (Ea) of the catalytic system was derived as 27.2 kJ/mol using Arrhenius equation.

The reusability is also a significant property of catalyst. The CDs/Fe3O4@CS catalyst could be easily separated from the reaction solution using a magnet. The nanocomposites showed high ibuprofen degradation efficiencies and rates in the five successive degradation cycle experiments (Fig. 7 ). There was only 5.6% loss of ibuprofen degradation removal of five repetitive catalyst usage in 90 min comparable to the fresh one. The reusability without visible light illumination was not as good as with light (Fig. S8) might be due to the leakage of the Fe since the intensity of 5th run catalyst XRD spectra reduced compared to the fresh one according to Fig. S9. The reusability with visible light illumination was much better than the one without illumination, which might be due to the fact that the upconversion photo-catalysis properties of CDs did not change due to its chemical inertness to oxidation. The upconversion property enables the CD-based photo-catalysts to be workable under visible light induction [22]. The free radicals might also be generated through scission of the peroxide bond induced by CD upconversion light illumination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of reactive species and possible ibuprofen degradation pathways
ce:section.ce:para: DMPO and TEMP were separately used as ESR spin-trapping agents to identify the reactive species in the CDs/Fe3O4@CS activated persulfate system and the results are illustrated in Fig. 8 . The hyperfine splitting constants (AN = 14.8 G, AH = 14.8 G) of DMPO·OH can be observed in Fig. 8a(1) and b(1), which indicates the generation of hydroxyl radical under both conditions (with visible light and without light illumination). The presence of sulfate radical was difficult to determine by ESR using DMPO, which was due to the low fluxes of this radical in the system and the low reaction rate between SO4 ·- and DMPO (0.027 M−1s−1). In addition, DMPO·SO4 - adduct can be rapidly converted to DMPO·OH adduct by nucleophilic substitution [1,40]. Excess tert-butyl alcohol was added before catalysis reaction to eliminate the strong influence of hydroxyl radical [1]. The hyperfine splitting constants (AN = 13.8 G, AH = 10.1 G, Aγ1 H = 1.4 G and Aγ2 H = 0.8 G) could be identified in the CDs/Fe3O4@CS activated persulfate systems with excess TBA as shown in both Fig. 8a(2) and b(2), which is consistent with the characteristics of DMPO·SO4 - [41,42]. Furthermore, four peak heights of Fig. 8a(2) are similar, which is also consistent with the characteristics of DMPO·-SO4 -. Ibuprofen could be efficiently eliminated in the CDs/Fe3O4@CS activated persulfate system via attacking by sulfate and hydroxyl radicals, which have high redox potentials and react with target compound through electron transfer, hydrogen abstraction and/or addition reaction [3].

The characteristic ESR spectra of 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidone-N-oxide (TEMPO) with hyperfine coupling constants (AN = 16.7 G) could be easily observed as shown in Fig. 8a(3) and b(3) when TEMP was used as an ESR spin-trapping reagent in this system. Therefore, the generation of singlet oxygen can be proved in the CDs/Fe3O4@CS activated persulfate system. Singlet oxygen can react with electron-rich double bonds and other singlet-state compounds due to its high electrophilicity and oxidizing ability [43–46]. Therefore, singlet oxygen generated in the CDs/Fe3O4@CS activated persulfate system might promote the ibuprofen removal efficiencies and accelerate the degradation reaction rates via an electron transfer reaction with ibuprofen degradation intermediates.

The non-radical mechanism were identified in carbon nanotube [47] and mesoporous carbon [48] activated persulfate. The radical quenching or inhibition experiments in present of excess radical scavengers were also conducted in this work to discriminate non-radical mechanism as shown in Fig. S10. The ibuprofen removal efficiency was reduced to less than 3% with TBA and the ibuprofen removal efficiency was reduced to less than 57% with methanol, which similar to adsorption amount of CDs/Fe3O4@CS. The radical quenching results indicating that the radical degradation processes play a dominant role in the CDs/Fe3O4@CS activated persulfate system.

The ibuprofen degradation intermediates in the CDs/Fe3O4@CS activated persulfate system were identified using LC-MS in ESI positive and negative ion scan model (Fig. 9 ) and selective ion monitoring model (S11). The detailed chemical information for the intermediates, such as the retention time and molecular weight, is listed in Table S2. The para propionic acid and isobutyl groups of ibuprofen underwent different reaction processes individually before aromatic opening and further mineralization, so two main degradation pathways (I-II) were proposed and are illustrated in Fig. 10 according to the LC-MS results and previous literatures [49–52]. In the pathway I, the propionic acid groups of ibuprofen first underwent hydroxylation and decarboxylation due to the attack of sulfate and hydroxyl radicals resulting in compound P1, which followed three sub-pathways (III ~ V). In pathway III, P2 was produced through the dehydration reaction of P1. P4 could be generated via the hydrogen abstraction of P3 by sulfate and hydroxyl radicals, and P4 also could be directly produced from P2 by addition reaction by singlet oxygen, which is similar to the previous report [53]. In pathway IV, P1 could be transformed to P5 via deprotonation reaction, which could further produce P6 through hydroxylation at the C(1) position of the benzene ring and loss of acetic acid [50]. In pathway V, ibuprofen might convert to P7 via demethylation and hydroxylation reaction and P8 could be subsequently produced through deprotonation reaction [51]. In pathway II, P1 could be transformed to P9 through hydroxylation and P10 could be subsequently generated through deprotonation reaction. The cleavage of the isobutyl moiety of P10 formed P11. The compound P12 could be produced via the hydroxylation of P10 and P13 was generated through the decarboxylation of P11 [52]. Finally, aromatic rings were opened by radical attack and the intermediates were mineralized to inorganic compounds, such as water and carbon dioxide.

CDs/Fe3O4@CS pomegranate-like composites were successfully synthesized via a solvothermal method. CDs/Fe3O4@CS exhibited high catalytic activities for three peroxides and all components of the nanocomposites might contribute to ibuprofen degradation. Pomegranate-like CDs/Fe3O4@CS are promising for persulfate activation with high catalytic activity, good reusability and low activation energy. The existence of singlet oxygen, hydroxyl and sulfate radicals was also proved to attribute for ibuprofen degradation. Pomegranate-like nanocomposites have the potential in the cleaner pollution control applications and more works might be stimulated based on the results of this work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Key R & D Program of China (2018YFD0900805), the Beijing Natural Science Foundation (8202029), the Beijing Advanced Innovation Program for Land Surface Science and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 41831283 and 21307005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2020.116820.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
